svg.text: The work of operating capabilities that state, local, territorial, and tribal health departments need but can't always build or sustain on their own.
PHIs provide the staff, infrastructure, and operational reach that lets thin governmental public health do more than it could alone.
Good delivery builds delivery reputation, which wins HD
contracts over vendors, which funds the staff bench, which
delivers more. The flywheel of the function.
Public trust unlocks federal funding, which builds the
workforce, which raises health departments' ability to
The same federal cuts that hollow out health departments
and raise their demand for the bridge also shrink the
bridge's own funding and bench. Demand up, capacity down,
Staff are hired against specific grants, so when a grant
ends the people and the capability leave together. The
reputation and capability stocks never fully compound.
Health departments can buy surge and TA from commercial
vendors instead. When PHI capacity or trust falls, the
work shifts to vendors who keep no field knowledge.

text.tspan: The flywheel: good delivery wins the contracts that fund the next round.
Public trust to funding to capacity to results to trust. The slow outer loop.
Demand up, capacity down, same dollar.

g.text: raise HD demand for the bridge (+)
but the same cuts shrink supply (−)

text.tspan: Earmarked, time-limited money keeps durable capacity from compounding.
Reputation is the moat. Lose it and the work shifts to commercial vendors.

g.text: work shifts to vendors, no field knowledge (+)

svg.text: what the whole subsystem turns on
Health departments can get surge and TA from PHIs or from
commercial vendors. So trust in PHIs and the friction of using
them is what decides where the work goes.
Delivery reputation is the moat (R1). Fragility and the grant-bench
trap (B1, B2) erode supply; vendor substitution (B3) captures the
work when they do. Reputation is built slowly and spent fast.
